coredata.dc:title: A luminescent mesoporous zirconium complex and it as a precursor of environmentally benign catalysts
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Gong-Yi
Chen, Yu-Li
Xu, Shi
coredata.dc:description: Much attention should be paid to developing new materials for energy saving and environmentally benign catalysis. Herein we report the broad-band direct white-light emission originating from a single-phased and single-doped phosphor,i.e., a luminescent mesoporous zirconium oxy-hydroxy acetate–persulfate complex and the complex doped by Eu3+. The amorphous complexes exhibit the photoluminescence and color properties comparable to or better than those of the crystalline MOFs. The attracting feature of this route to white-light emission comes from the single-phased and single-doped phosphors that can be prepared through a low-cost chemical synthesis approach. In addition to photoluminescence, the complexes show useful textural properties. The pristine complex has been used as a precursor of environmentally benign catalysts in the esterification of lauric acid with methanol and in n-hexane isomerization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
White-light
Mesopore
Esterification
Isomerization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra (a),fluorescence lifetime(b), and CIE chromaticity diagram (c) of the pristine complex.
Photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra (a),fluorescence lifetime (b), and CIE chromaticity diagram (c) of the Eu3+-doped complex.
Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and corresponding pore size distributions of the pristine complex (a,b) and Eu3+-doped complex (c,d).
Photoluminescence and color properties of recent organic-inorganic hybrid materials as a single-phased and single-doped phosphor for white-light emitting.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Sandia Metal-Organic Framework-1(SMOF-1), namely[In3(btb)2(oa)3]n, (btb = 1,3,5-tris(4-carboxyphenyl) benzene, oa = oxalic acid).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: g
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Value in parentheses represents lifetime distribution[%].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of catalytic activity of our catalyst with other related reported systems for the esterification of lauric acid with methanol.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Methanol to lauric acid molar ratio.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Catalyst amount to lauric acid mass ratio.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Methanol to lauric acid molar ratio.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: f
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Catalyst amount to lauric acid mass ratio.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the experimental conditions and results of recent studies with our work for n-hexane isomerization.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: j
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Catalyst is Pt-d30MFI(300), Pt loaded over the zeolite desilicated with NaOH.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: l
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Catalyst is three metals(Pt,La,Al)-promoted sulfated zirconia.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: n
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Catalyst is 0.1M NaOH-treated Pt/HMOR, HMOR = protonic mordenite.

head.ce:title: A luminescent mesoporous zirconium complex and it as a precursor of environmentally benign catalysts

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Dong Chuan Road 800, Shanghai 200240, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shang Da Road, Shanghai 200444, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instrumental Analysis Center, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Dong Chuan Road 800, Shanghai 200240, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Much attention should be paid to developing new materials for energy saving and environmentally benign catalysis. Herein we report the broad-band direct white-light emission originating from a single-phased and single-doped phosphor,i.e., a luminescent mesoporous zirconium oxy-hydroxy acetate–persulfate complex and the complex doped by Eu3+. The amorphous complexes exhibit the photoluminescence and color properties comparable to or better than those of the crystalline MOFs. The attracting feature of this route to white-light emission comes from the single-phased and single-doped phosphors that can be prepared through a low-cost chemical synthesis approach. In addition to photoluminescence, the complexes show useful textural properties. The pristine complex has been used as a precursor of environmentally benign catalysts in the esterification of lauric acid with methanol and in n-hexane isomerization.

Amorphous zirconium oxy-hydroxy acetate–persulfate complex and the complex doped by Eu3+ have been developed as single-phased and single-doped phosphors for white-light emission. They exhibit the photoluminescence and color properties comparable to or better than those of the crystalline MOFs. In addition, the pristine complex can be used as a precursor of environmentally benign catalysts in the esterification of lauric acid with methanol and in n-hexane isomerization.

ce:section.ce:para: With the emerging global energy crisis and the concerns of global warming, development of light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with high luminous efficiency will have significant impact on energy conservation. Presently there are two pathways to achieve white-light emission: (1) the combination of two or more phosphors, i.e., either by coating a yellow phosphor or by combining green and red phosphors on a background consisting of a blue-light emitting diode or by employing nanocrystals of three primary colors (red, green, blue) using multilayer structures in LED; (2) a single phosphor with a broad emission spectrum. The main disadvantages for the first method are the necessary for a homogeneous and reproducible phosphor mixture, and the often different thermal behavior of the phosphors. However, the use of single-phased full-color emitting phosphors for near ultraviolet chip-based white-light emitting diode offers many advantages over multiple component systems, for example, greater luminous efficiency and reproducibility, and low cost preparation, due to avoidance of color-aberration and simpler fabrication processes. It is, therefore, an excellent option to develop a single-phased white-light emitting phosphor using the principle of energy transfer from a sensitizer (energy donor) to an activator (energy acceptor) in a single host lattice, in other words, multidoped luminescent ions in one matrix [1]. Although myriads of new phosphors have been recently reported, it was indicated in a critical review that the number of currently available conversion phosphors fulfilling all the following requirements is still limited [2]. Main requirements for conversion phosphors are 1. good excitability in the near-UV to blue region of the spectrum, namely, the excitation spectrum should be sufficiently broad to cover the wavelength range; 2. a broad emission spectrum that can lead to a white-light emission with a high color rendering and warm color temperature; 3. absence of emission saturation at high fluxes. In addition, the fluorescent materials should exhibit structural, thermal, and photochemical stability in aggressive environments while showing high absorption coefficient and quantum yield with minimal toxicity. Even if a phosphor gets high marks on all these requirements, the actual device development involves several other considerations such as the ease and environmental friendliness of a synthesis method and ultimately also the cost of the material. Research into photoluminescent materials should therefore continue. One of the most exciting advances in the field of multifunctional materials in recent years is the emergence of a fascinating family of organic-inorganic hybrid materials, known as metal-organic frameworks(MOFs). Metal-organic-frameworks (MOFs) or porous coordination polymers are crystalline porous materials. In MOFs, metal complexes form vertices of the structure, connected by organic linker. This architecture allow one, through selection of appropriate linkers, to create extremely sparse materials with record low crystal densities and ultrahigh surface areas, which can be applied to gas adsorption and storage, catalysis, and drug delivery. Additionally fuelling interest in MOFs is their luminescence properties. MOFs offer the advantages of the choice of light-emission building block for both the metal clusters as nodes and the functional organic ligands as spokes; furthermore, the spatial regularity of these building blocks prevents the preferential aggregation that causes self-quenching. A combination of porosity and luminescence (MOFs) can be used in everyday life, e.g., solid-state lighting as well as in highly specialized applications such as optoelectronic and photonic devices, chemical and biological sensing, molecular tracing, and cellular imaging.

Direct white-light emission has only been observed in a few coordination compounds [3]. Recently, organic charge–transfer complexes [4], supramolecular gels [5], and organic cations-templated layered perovskites [6] have been developed for white-light emitting, but their photoluminescent quantum yield and thermal stability are major constraints to be overcome for optoelectronic and lighting applications. Herein we report the broad-band direct white-light emission originating from a single-phased and single-doped phosphor, i.e., a luminescent mesoporous zirconium oxy-hydroxyl acetate–persulfate complex and the complex doped by Eu 3+ (10wt%). The complexes can be synthesized from zirconyl chloride, acetic acid, and ammonium persulfate under structure-directing agents-free at a temperature as low as 210 °C. The amorphous complexes exhibit the photoluminescence and color properties comparable to or better than those of the crystalline MOFs. In addition to photoluminescence, the complexes show useful textural properties. The pristine complex has been used as a precursor of environmentally benign catalysts in the esterification of lauric acid with methanol and in n-hexane isomerization.

ZrOCl2·8H2O or its mixture with EuCl3 aqueous solution followed by aqueous ammonia was dropwise added to a boiled and cooled mixture of water, acetic acid and ammonium persulfate until pH 4.9 for catalyst or pH 5.5 for phosphor. The resultant precipitate was aged in the mother liquor overnight, and then kept in a boiling water bath for 2 h. The precipitate was washed with deionized water, ammonia/absolute alcohol (1:1), and only absolute alcohol, and dried at 100 °C for 48 h for catalyst or at 210 °C for phosphor. The dried precipitate also called phosphor looked yellowish. It was impregnated by an incipient wetness method with 30 gl−1 ammonium persulfate solution for 30 min, dried at 100 °C for 15 h, and calcined at 650°Cfor3 h, yielding sulfated zirconia (SZ). The palladium(around 0.5wt%) was incorporated on the SZ calcined at 650 °C by classical impregnation using an acidic solution of Pd. After drying at 120 °C for 13 h, the catalyst was calcined in air at 450 °C for 3 h.

The catalytic properties of samples in an acid-catalyzed reaction (n-hexane isomerization) were studied in a continuous fixed-bed reactor under atmospheric pressure and at 180 °C, with a molar ratio of H2/n-C6 = 2.0 in the feed. n-Hexane was fed into the reactor with a syringe pump with a rate of 2 mlh−1, while hydrogen flowed at 60 ml min−1 1 g catalyst was taken and placed between two section of sand beads in the reactor tube. Prior to reaction, the catalyst was pretreated at 300 °C for 40 min in H2(60 ml min−1). n-Hexane flew first through a trap filled with 4A zeolite to remove impurities then through the catalyst bed at weight hourly space velocity (WHSV = 1.0 h−1).The reaction mixture was analyzed by on-line gas chromatograph. Besides iso-hexane, minor amounts of cracking products were formed. The conversions are those measured at 25 min time on stream.

Esterification was preformed in a stirred round bottom flask from where samples were withdrawn periodically for analysis on a gas chromatograph equipped with a capillary column and a flame ionization detector. Esterification was carried out at 60 °C using 0.16 g of catalyst, 4.167 g of lauric acid and 10 g of methanol.

For the acidity measurements, the sample was pressed into a disk (ca.30 mg, 1.5 cm diameter), and treated in an IR cell equipped with CaF2 windows under vacuum conditions (5 × 10−3 torr) at 200 °C for 4 h and then cooled to room temperature. Pyridine is widely used to identify the concentration and nature of acid sites on the surface of sulfated zirconia. Pyridine-saturated vapor was adsorbed on the sample disk at room temperature for15 min followed by thermal desorption from room temperature to 300 °C under vacuum.

IR spectra for pyridine adsorption and desorption testes were obtained with a Nicolet-5700 spectrophotometer at 4 cm−1 resolution, and were a result of 32 co-added scans. Spectra were presented in the form of absorbance, and difference FTIR spectra were obtained by subtracting the spectrum of the catalyst from that of the sample with adsorbed pyridine.

Surface area, pore size distribution, and total pore volume measurements were performed in an automated nitrogen adsorption-desorption apparatus (Micromeritics ASAP 2010 or ASAP 2020) at liquid nitrogen temperature 77 K using nitrogen as the adsorbate. Prior to measurement, the sample was degassed in vacuo at 200°Cfor 2 h. Most important textural parameters were calculated from nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms following to a large extent the methodology proposed by IUPAC. Surface area was calculated with the BET equation using a value of 0.162 nm2 for the cross-sectional area of nitrogen molecule from the adsorption branch of the isotherm in a relative pressure P/P0 range of 0.07–0.3. Total pore volume was computed based on the amount of N2 adsorbed at a relative pressure close to unity. Pore size distribution was estimated with the well-known BJH method from the desorption branch.

The diffused reflectance UV-VIS spectra of the sulfated zirconia and phosphors were recorded on a Varian Cary 5000 UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere attachment. The computer processing of the absorption spectrum was done with Cary I-E software that allowed calculation of the of the Kubelka-Munk function F(R) from the absorbance data. The band gap energy (Eg) for an allowed transition was determined by finding the intercept of the straight line in a plot of [F(R) X hυ]2 versus hυ, where hυ is the incident photon energy. Or, the absorption edge energy was determined for the spectrum by a linear extrapolation to zero absorption.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by mixing the sample with KBr (≧99% purity) in a !:10 ratio of sample to KBr and pressing into a pellet on a model QM/TM/IM spectrophotometer (Photon Technology International) equipped with a nitrogen laser. The excitation spectra were collected by scanning from 310 to490 nm under monitoring the emission at 490 nm at a slit width of 2 nm. The emission spectra were scanned from 451 to 535 nm at a slit width of 1 nm. Temperature dependence of the photoluminescence was measured in the temperature range from 25 to 125 °C. Transient PL decays were measured by a nitrogen laser (λ = 337 nm, 10 Hz) combined with a streak scope C 4334 and a synchronous decay generator C4792-02 at an excitation wavelength of 429 nm while monitoring the emission at 521 nm. The PL decays were analyzed by fitting with a multiexponential function f(t) using the software (Felix 32):f(t) = ∑Ai exp(−t/τi) + B, where Ai is the decay amplitude, B is a constant (the baseline offset), and τi is the time constant of the decay i. Photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) along with CIE chromaticity coordinates were measured using phosphor powders encapsulated in a Teflon mount equipped with a transparent quartz cap inside an integrating sphere and excited using 370–410 nm light generated by a xenon lamp in Jasco FP 6600 spectrophotometer or Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluormeter. Also, CIE chromaticity coordinates along with color rendering index and correlated color temperature were determined on SPR920 Spectroradiometric & Colorimeter (Zhejiang University Sensing Instruments Co.).

Zirconium and other metals, carbon and sulfur, and chlorine in samples were determined by inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy(ICP-AES) or-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [Thermo Scientific iCAP 6000 series ICP Spectrometer] and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy(EDXS) on scanning electron microscopy(SEM), elemental analysis [Elementar Vario ELIII], and X-ray fluorescence analysis, respectively.

The excitation and emission spectra of the pristine complex are displayed in Fig. 1 , along with fluorescence lifetime and CIE chromaticity diagram. In the excitation spectra monitoring the emission at 521 nm, a broad excitation band peaking at 429 nm with the full width at half maximum (FWHM) 125 nm is observed, which is attributed to the Zr–O charge transfer band. The emission spectrum is characterized by a single, distinct, and broad emission band peaking at 521 nm upon excitation at 429 nm. The full width at half maximum of the emission peak is broader than 131 nm, which allows for good color rendering. One of the essential conditions usually required for energy transfer is the overlap between the donor emission and the acceptor excitation spectra. Such emission spectrum implies that the pristine complex can realize near white-light emission with CIE chromaticity coordinates (0.282, 0.339), color rendering index (CRI) 79.6, and correlated color temperature (CCT) 8054K. The absolute quantum yield (QY) was found to be 8.83% when excited at 410 nm, which is comparable to those of the MOFs in Table 1 .

The material luminesces broadly in the visible light region because of its large bandgap 5.38 ev, as derived from the UV–Vis absorption data(see Fig. S1 in the supporting information). The emission is similar to that observed in other luminescent carbon materials, the exact origin of which is still controversial. In our case it could be due to excitonic recombination and defects, e.g., hydrocarbon-based defect originating from the acetic acid to prepare the pristine complex and carbon impurity arising from the decomposition of a minor amount of organic species at 210 °C. The above discussion is supported by photoluminescence lifetime in nanosecond range and excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior(see Fig. S2 in the supporting information), because these also are the properties possessed by luminescent carbon materials such as carbon dots [7] and fluographene [8].

The PL spectrum of the Eu3+–doped complex excited at 392 nm shows several strong emission bands peaking at 467, 590, and 613 nm at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The emission at 467 nm is attributed to excitonic recombination and defects as in the case of the pristine complex. The emission bands at 613 and 590 nm are assigned to the 5D0 – 7Fj (j = 2 or 1) electric or magnetic dipole transitions of the Eu3+ ions from excited 5D0 level to ground-state levels 7F2 or 1, respectively [9,10]. The inclusion of 5d levels in the photoluminescence process implies broad-band transitions, as the ground and excited states differ as a function of the distance of the electrons from the core. The emission maximum is thereby dependent on the chemical surroundings, and thus on the coordination sphere of the europium centers [9,11]. Generally speaking, the broad blue-to-red emission are very important to achieve white-light emission of single-component material. The complex doped by Eu3+ under UV excitation at 365 nm exhibits a white-light emission with CIE chromaticity coordinates(0.3405,0.3562), color rendering index(94.5), and correlated color temperature (5192 K). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the complex is 14.13% when excited by 392 nm light, which is higher than those of the MOFs in Table 1. The intense luminescence of the complex is attributable to the strong interaction between the ligand and metal, or the increasing rigidity due to the chelating effect of the ligand, which effectively reduces the loss of energy by nonradiative decay. The distinct hard-and-soft characters of the carboxyl and sulfur groups exist in the complexes studied:the ionic, chemically hard carboxyl group tends to bind metal ions for network formation, while the softer sulfur groups could remain as free-standing, secondary donor groups. Therefore, the combination of carboxylic and sulfur functions makes it a potentially powerful strategy for the molecular design of coordination networks [12], yielding the intense photoluminescence in the present case.

The luminescence decay, i.e., lifetime is an important parameter related to the quality of material, and is mainly determined by the concentration of defects, which can trap the electrons or holes and have an influence on the recombination process of excitons. Fig. 1b shows the photoluminescence decay profile of the pristine complex. The decay curve was measured and recorded for 521 nm emission at 429 nm excitation by a time-correlated single-photon counting technique at room temperature. The decay curves are well fitted to a double-exponential function as I (t) = A1 exp (−t/τ1) + A2 exp (−t/τ2), where τ1 and τ2 are the decay lifetimes of the luminescence, and A1 and A2 are weighting parameters [9]. The decay curve is composed of one fast and one slow component, revealing that the luminescence decay can be associated with two different probabilities of radiative decay. The observed lifetimes are τ1 = 2.11 ns and τ2 = 7.23 ns. In order to prevent saturation in high flux devices, fast decay times are necessary [2]. The band-edge emission is quenched and thus a faster decay results, while the trap-associated recombination becomes slower as is apparent at the tail end of the decay profiles. A long decay time is characteristic of most symmetrical surroundings. When poor crystallinity exists, the excitation energy is non-radiatively dissipated into the host lattice, resulting in short luminescence lifetime. Fig. 2b shows the emission decay curves of the Eu3+-doped complex by monitoring the emission at 467, 590, and 613 nm, respectively. The decay curves are well fitted with a single exponential function as II0 + A1 exp(−t/τ1) where I and I0 are phosphorescence intensity, A1 is constant and t is time, τ1 is decay time for exponential component, yielding the lifetimes of 1.525, 1.838 and 1.806 ns for the emission at 467, 590, and 613 nm, respectively.

In high-power packages of white LEDs, the LED junction temperature can be up to 150 °C.Consequently, temperature dependence of luminescence intensity should be investigated. The temperature dependence of luminescence intensity for the pristine complex was investigated in the temperature from 25 to 105 °C under the excitation of 429 nm at an intervals 40 °C with a holding time of 5 min at each temperature. The obtained results were presented in Fig. S3. With increasing temperature up to 105 °C, it gradually decreased to 57% of that measured at 20 °C. Interestingly, luminescence intensity can be recovered after cooling. In addition, no obvious shift is observed for the position of the emission band when the temperature rises, suggesting the stable chromaticity coordinates of this phosphor by varying the temperature.

In addition to photoluminescence, the above complexes exhibit useful textural properties. Fig. 3 a and c present nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms at 77 K for the complexes studied. According to the IUPAC classification [15], the shape of the isotherms resembles the type IV isotherm with H1 hysteresis loop at high relative pressure, indicating capillary condensation taking place in mesopores. The shape and sharpness of the isotherms revealed the mesoporous feature of the complexes. They had an average pore diameter in the mesoporous range, 11.71 nm for the pristine complex and 7.23 nm for the Eu3+-doped complex, as shown in the BJH pore distribution Fig. 3b and d. The pristine complex and the Eu3+-doped complex possess a BET surface area of about 216 and 210 m2g−1 and total pore volume of 0.467 and 0.379 cm3g−1, respectively. The high surface area also implies that the complexes are highly porous. Therefore, the pristine complex can be used as a precursor of environmentally benign catalysts.

Homogeneous, corrosive liquid acid catalysts, such as H2SO4, HCl and complexes of BF3 are frequently used in organic syntheses. However, the organic syntheses involving these conventional acids are inherently associated with problems such as high toxicity, corrosion, tedious work-up procedure, long reaction times, low selectivity, and large amounts of catalysts, which eventually result in the generation of large amounts of toxic waste. Therefore, discovery of efficient and environmentally benign catalysts is one of the most compelling objectives of modern chemistry. Solid catalysts, for example, sulfated metal oxides have been developed as environmentally friendly replacements for mineral acid catalysts because they possess very high acidic strength and hence exhibit high catalytic activity for various acid-catalyzed reactions. Besides the acidity, the textural parameters such as specific surface area, and pore diameter and volume of a catalyst also contribute to the catalyst activity. A higher specific surface area favors the spacing of the sulfate groups, leading to less dense, more accessible and a large number of total acid sites. It is clear that the most acid sites are in line with the fact that a catalyst has great surface area and sulfate content. The large pore diameter and volume can make the reaction more feasible due to easy diffusion of reactants and product molecules. The textural properties of a catalyst are largely determined by preparation method including the initial precursor used as well as the post-synthesis treatment, e.g., digestion, calcination temperature and impregnation. The conventional preparation method for sulfated zirconia(SZ) results in microporous material. There are two pathways or mechanisms for the synthesis of mesoporous zirconia: templating mechanism and scaffolding mechanism. However, removal of a template by calcination or extraction often cause the material formed to collapse. If a mesoporous zirconia was synthesized by using surfactants, the material formed had broad pore size distributions that not be suitable for the shape-selective catalytic reactions of large molecules. Herein, we report the sulfated zirconia synthesized under structure-directing agent-free and calcined at 650 °C, which can show a high BET surface area (83 m2g−1), mesopore (18 nm), and large total pore volume (0.42 cm3g−1). In addition, monometallic Pd-promoted sulfated zirconia remains mesoporous, with surface area 91 m2g−1, averaged pore diameter 12 nm, and total pore volume 0.26 cm3g−1. In the following tests, the sulfated zirconia treated with silver sulfate and Pd-promoted sulfated zirconia were used as environmentally benign catalysts for the esterification of lauric acid with methanol and for n-hexane isomerization, respectively.

Table 2 compares the activities of various catalysts studied recently in the open literature for the esterification of lauric acid with methanol. As can be seen from the Table 2, in our work the conversion of lauric acid increased gradually with reaction time. Nearly 63% conversion of lauric acid was observed in just 30 min and near complete conversion 99% of it was achieved after 3 h at temperatures as low as 60 °C. Methyl laurate was the only reaction product except water, indicating that no sulfuric acid was leached from the catalyst and the selectivity was 100%. The sulfated zirconia with sulfur loading 11 wt% exhibited a quite high acidity, which are primarily responsible for rapid reaction leading to a high yield of the product.

Environmental concerns have promoted legislation to limit the amount of aromatics in gasoline. However, reduction of aromatics has a negative impact on gasoline octane number that has to be compensated by other approaches. Alkanes is environmentally more acceptable than aromatics, and the branched hydrocarbons has higher octane numbers than linear alkane. The skeletal isomerization of n-alkanes plays an important role in the production of branched, high-octane hydrocarbons as a replacement of traditional tetra-ethyl lead additives. Isomerization of normal pentane (n-C5) and hexane (n-C6) has been recognized to be one of the most economical units to boost octane level especially for the lower boiling gasoline. In our work, a monometallic Pd-promoted sulfated zirconia was used for n-hexane isomerization.

The n-hexane isomerization of the catalyst has been investigated by running the reaction at 180 °C under atmospheric pressure in a continuous fixed-bed reactor. The initial conversion on the catalyst is 65%, because an induction period was involved at the beginning of the reaction, followed by a maximum conversion 75%, S (2,2-DMB) 15%, S (i-C6) 95%, as shown in Table 3 , and the remaining constant at ca.77% conversion, 14%(2,2-DMB), 94.8%S(i-C6) after 4 h. It is well known that the reutilization of solid catalysts is a key property. The activity of our catalyst decayed gradually with time on stream, but all activities can be completely restored by thermal treatment in air at 450 °C for 1.5 h. More Interesting, the reused catalyst (entry3 in Table 3)may show a higher C6 conversion83% and S(2,2DMB)18%, than the conversion75% and S(2,2-DMB)15% of the fresh catalyst (entry 2 in Table 3). The increased Brønsted acidity together with unique structuring of species on the sulfated zirconia studied as well as the unique pore structure that consisted of a combination of the major amounts of mesopores and minor amounts of macropores at the same time, as shown in Fig. S4(see supporting information), may be responsible for the high activity in the n-hexane isomerization.

The trend in the literature is to recognize a decrease in catalyst surface area and pore volume and therefore identify the most likely catalyst deactivation mechanism as coking and organic deposits. However this remains contentious. In the present work, after regeneration the catalyst exhibits nearly complete recovery of surface area and pore volume, and a higher Pd content, as shown in Table 3, and hence a corresponding significant recovery of isomerization activity. One very important reason of deactivation of the sulfated zirconia may be attributed to a loss of sulfates, as shown in Table 3.

We report a luminescent mesoporous zirconium oxy-hydroxyl acetate–persulfate complex and the complex doped by Eu3+. As can be seen from Table 1, the amorphous complexes exhibit the photoluminescence and color properties comparable to or better than those of the crystalline MOFs, suggesting that the complexes are potential candidates for white-light emission. The attracting feature of this route to white-light emission comes from the single-phased and single-doped phosphors that can be prepared through a low-cost chemical synthesis approach from zirconyl chloride, acetic acid, and ammonium persulfate at a temperature as low as 210 °C without incurring the burden of intrinsic toxicity and without the need for stringent, intricate, tedious, or costly preparation steps. It is, however, obvious that crystalline MOFs or white-light-emitting polymers are synthetically challenging, and environmental issues should also be evaluated due to use of large amount of environment-friendlyless organic solvents [23]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time we are reporting this amorphous organic complex as a potential candidate for single-phased and single-doped white-light–emitting phosphor. In addition to photoluminescence, the complexes show useful textural properties. As can be seen from Fig. 2, the pristine complex and Eu3+-doped complex have an average pore size of 11.71 and 7.23 nm, specific surface area of 216 and 210 m2 g−1, and pore volume of 0.467 and 0.379 cm3g−1, respectively. Therefore, the pristine complex can be used as a precursor of environmentally benign catalysts.

It is obvious from Table 3 that the Pd-promoted sulfated zirconia prepared without any structure-directing agents in this study has higher surface area, much larger pore volume and pore size than those reported in the literature under similar preparation conditions. It has been well accepted that these are important factors in determining the better catalyst. Tables 2 and 3 compares the activities of various catalysts studied in the open literature for the esterification of lauric acid with methanol and for n-hexane isomerization, respectively. The sulfated zirconia reported here showed best performance among different types of acid catalysts. It may be regarded as an important advancement in sulfated zirconia preparation and application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Prof. Chang-Lin. Chen at College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Technology and Prof. Weiming Hua at Department of Chemistry, Fudan University, for help with the n-hexane isomerization and with the esterification of lauric acid and methanol, respectively. We are grateful to Dr. Manager Director Jicang Guo at Guy Carpenter & Company Pty Ltd. for assistance in preparing figures and tables.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.micromeso.2015.04.013.
